[176:42]third party messaging service. And ostensibly that means that it could be hacked. That means

[176:49]that the information being communicated is not secured. So it could be going out to potentially

[220:22]There was just a kind of ethos

[220:25]where

[220:28]You just show up for your friends, you know like that that kind of camaraderie and loyalty

[220:36]Was such an important part of that social scene in the city. I don't want to say my dad was like a gangbanger

[220:52]You know, it's like the Warriors you ever see the that movie the Warriors

[220:56]That's obviously like a ridiculous movie, but you know, you had these like social scenes and different neighborhoods and

[221:03]There was like this deep sense of camaraderie

[221:05]if you came from the same neighborhood,

[221:07]if you grew up together,

[221:08]you went to the same high school or whatever.

[221:18]it wasn't some corny ass thing where he's like,

[221:20]you know, giving me like a life lesson,

[221:22]like out of a movie.

[221:23]But that was like the context in which I grew up.

[221:27]Like that's how my, that's how my father is.

[221:33]you know, friendship is such an important thing.

[221:37]And, you know, when you have a friend,

[221:41]if your friend kills somebody,

[221:44]you help them bury the body

[221:45]and then you ask questions later.

[221:49]but that was the kind of mindset,

[221:51]that was the kind of idea.

[221:56]if that's like a neighborhood thing,

[221:57]if you grew up in the neighborhoods in Chicago

[221:59]or it's a city thing, maybe it's an ethnic thing.

[222:01]But that was the kind of ethos I grew up with.

[222:05]That's what I grew up with from both my parents.

[222:08]That was the idea is if you have friends,

[222:11]that really means something.

[222:13]That's an important thing.

[222:14]You show up for your friends, you're loyal to them.

[224:03]That's something that's very important to me.

[224:05]That's a value I hold very dear.

[224:09]And for as long as I've been doing this, people have, you know, they want me to disavow my

[224:15]That's been like a theme in my career.

[224:20]Are you going to disavow them?

[224:37]want me to do something for them.

[224:51]oh, now they don't wanna be my friend.

[224:59]I won't be Nick's friend because I got in trouble for it.

[225:04]Now, before that happened,

[225:05]he wanted to be my best good friend.

[225:07]We were hanging out, he wanted to do favors for me,

[225:08]wanted me on a show,

[225:12]And then the second it got hot,

[225:16]And I get it, like I get it,

[225:17]but I have so few friends for that reason.

[225:21]And so, you know, that's why that's a very important thing

[225:25]And that's another reason why it's important to me.

[225:29]So that's why I don't take it lightly when people say, oh, you know, so and so did this

[225:34]said that.

[225:35]Why are you still friends with them?

[225:36]It's like because

[225:38]We're all people, I'm not gonna disavow someone that I know

[225:43]and that I've met and engaged with and have rapport with

[225:46]because they've said something I disagree with

[225:49]because they're not a perfect person.

[225:52]I'm not a perfect person.

[225:53]If everyone was gonna disavow me

[225:57]because of an inconvenient thing that I've said or done

[226:00]or because I'm not perfect, I would have no friends.

[226:02]Nobody would have friends.

[226:11]I think the world in particular runs on men being friends with each other, because an

[226:16]individual can achieve so little, it's really bands, it's groups, it's small, solid core
